properties.description: UK legal framework governing working hours, rest periods, and shift patterns
Core warehouse throughput metric measuring picker productivity
Structured problem-solving methodology using Fishbone/Ishikawa diagrams and 5 Whys
Organising warehouse locations to minimise travel time and picking errors based on velocity and similarity risk
Percentage of orders picked correctly, key quality KPI
Key performance indicators for warehouse operations including LPH, accuracy, despatch compliance, returns rate, replenishment cycle time, staff utilisation
Structured approach to managing workforce absence including RTW interviews, Bradford Factor, welfare meetings
Operational shift structures including continental, annualised hours, and base rota designs
Formula for scoring short-term intermittent absence, S² × D, used to trigger formal absence procedures
Digital recording of agency and new starter induction completions for compliance and traceability
UK data protection and cookie law compliance framework for websites
Privacy-friendly cookieless analytics platform, PECR compliant without consent banner

nodes.key_insight: People were a contributing factor in 30% of errors. Process and Environment: over 70%. The operation was generating errors — not the operators.

nodes.label: Process — Top Root Cause Category (85%)

nodes.label: People — Root Cause Category (30%)
nodes.context: Lowest contributing factor; slide emphasises operation at fault, not operators

nodes.label: Key Insight: The Operation Was Generating Errors, Not the Operators

nodes.label: LPH Improvement Carousel — Slide 5: The Takeaway
nodes.author: Paulo Gomes
nodes.headline: Measurement changes behaviour faster than any training session.
nodes.body_text: We didn't retrain the team. We made performance visible — to them, not just to management. When people can see their own trend, they move it. Every time.
nodes.cta_text: Save this if your picking team's LPH isn't moving.
nodes.key_insight: Visibility of individual performance data drives behaviour change more effectively than formal training
nodes.topic: LPH improvement, warehouse picking, performance visibility, behaviour change

nodes.label: Measurement Drives Behaviour Change
nodes.description: The principle that making performance metrics visible to the individual changes their behaviour faster than training interventions

nodes.description: Sharing performance trend data directly with front-line workers, not just management

nodes.description: Key warehouse picking productivity metric: number of order lines picked per hour per picker

nodes.label: LPH Improvement Carousel — Slide 1 Hook
nodes.author: Paulo Gomes
nodes.hook_claim: I used to think fast pickers were just born that way. They weren't.
nodes.supporting_statement: Here's what 12 months of real data from a warehouse operation actually showed us.
nodes.narrative_role: hook — challenges assumption to create curiosity and drive swipes

nodes.label: LPH Improvement – Slide 2: The Situation (Context)
nodes.author: Paulo Gomes
nodes.headline: A picking team. Inconsistent LPH. All over the place.
key_points: Team average was masking a wide performance spread
Some operators significantly above average; others well below
The gap was not random — something was driving it
Hypothesis: if the driver could be identified, it could be replicated
nodes.topic: LPH performance variability in warehouse picking operations

nodes.label: Shift Comparison Carousel – Slide 5: Takeaway (Shift Culture Outweighs Individual Skill)
nodes.author: Paulo Gomes
nodes.headline: Shift culture outweighs individual skill.
nodes.body_text: Before you retrain your lower-performing shift, ask what conditions they're working in. Consistency, fewer interruptions, and peer accountability cost nothing to fix.
nodes.cta: Save this if your shifts are performing differently.
nodes.key_insight: Shift culture and working conditions outweigh individual skill in determining shift performance; fix consistency, interruptions, and accountability before retraining.

nodes.label: Top Performer Management — Slide 5: The Takeaway (Replicate What Top Performers Do)
nodes.author: Paulo Gomes
nodes.headline: Replicate what top performers do. Don't just celebrate them.
nodes.body: Every warehouse has a best operator. Most treat that as a personality trait. The ones who treat it as a process to document and spread — those are the operations that improve.
nodes.cta: Save this if you have a performance gap in your team.
nodes.key_insight: Top performer excellence should be treated as a repeatable process to document and spread, not a personality trait to admire.
nodes.norm_label: top performer management — slide 5: the takeaway (replicate what top performers do)

nodes.label: Top Performer Analysis – Hook Slide (Slide 1 of 5)
nodes.author: Paulo Gomes
nodes.headline: One operator was significantly above team average. We studied them.
nodes.subtext: Not to promote them. Not to celebrate them. To understand exactly what they were doing — and replicate it.
nodes.norm_label: top performer analysis – hook slide (slide 1 of 5)

nodes.label: Top Performer Post – Slide 02: The Situation (Performance Distribution Context)
nodes.author: Paulo Gomes
nodes.headline: The gap was too wide to be explained by talent alone.
nodes.body_summary: When mapping LPH by operator, one person consistently sat significantly above the team average while others clustered around the mean or were well below. Rather than giving a simple 'well done', the approach was to treat this top operator as a process to be documented.
nodes.norm_label: top performer post – slide 02: the situation (performance distribution context)

nodes.label: Concept: Performance Gap Too Wide to Be Talent Alone
nodes.description: The observation that a measurable performance outlier in a picking team exceeds what natural talent difference could account for, motivating process investigation and documentation.
nodes.norm_label: concept: performance gap too wide to be talent alone

nodes.label: Concept: LPH (Lines Per Hour) Distribution Mapping by Operator
nodes.description: The practice of mapping lines-per-hour productivity by individual operator to reveal performance distribution across a warehouse picking team.
nodes.norm_label: concept: lph (lines per hour) distribution mapping by operator

nodes.label: Concept: Treat Top Performer as a Process to Document
nodes.description: The strategic response to a performance outlier: instead of just acknowledging good work, systematically document what the top performer does differently so it can be replicated.

nodes.label: Induction Is Operational Preparation — The Takeaway (Slide 5 of 5)
nodes.author: Paulo Gomes
nodes.principle_heading: Induction is operational preparation. Not paperwork.
nodes.body_text: A signed form doesn't mean someone knows what to do when something goes wrong. Walk the exits. Confirm the PPE fits. Give them a name, not a title. Five things. Every starter. Every time.
nodes.cta_text: Save this for your next peak intake.
nodes.key_message: Induction must be treated as operational readiness, not a compliance checkbox — practical actions (walk exits, confirm PPE, use names) matter more than signed forms.
nodes.norm_label: induction is operational preparation — the takeaway (slide 5 of 5)

nodes.label: WMS Shelf Capacity — Slide 5: The Takeaway (Structural Misalignments Create Operator Errors)
nodes.author: Paulo Gomes
nodes.headline: Structural misalignments create errors that look like operator mistakes.
nodes.body_text: When did you last audit whether your WMS capacity figures match what your shelves actually hold? If it was at go-live — that's worth checking.
nodes.call_to_action: Save this if you're chasing errors you can't explain.
nodes.key_insight: WMS configuration errors that persist since go-live are often misattributed to operator mistakes rather than recognised as structural data misalignments
